source.title: Cerebral hierarchies: predictive processing, precision and the pulvinar.
source.venue: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences

confidence_rationale: The strongest direct hit in the anchor list. Kanai et al. argue that cortical hierarchies require a dedicated thalamic substrate (pulvinar) to deliver per-cortical-area modulatory gain control on prediction errors -- effectively, a per-region precision signal that selects which cortical streams contribute to ascending Bayesian inference. This is tag (a) direct cortex-side per-stream precision gating, biologically grounded in pulvinar circuitry, and explicitly framed as a route by which inference-content (driving, first-order) is dissociated from precision-context (modulatory, second-order). MECH-269b's V_s is essentially this precision channel, applied to E1 / E2 streams. Confidence 0.78 reflects high mapping fidelity for the cortex side and the explicit anatomical proposal; symmetric-application novelty (vs hippocampal proposer) remains unaddressed.
failure_signatures: Kanai et al. propose pulvinar-mediated precision gating but treat the precision parameter as set per cortical area, not per latent content stream within an area. MECH-269b's per-stream specialisation (z_world, z_self, z_harm_s independently gated) requires a finer granularity than this paper provides; the paper supplies an upper-bound substrate but does not demonstrate the specific resolution.

The hippocampal-cortical symmetry question is silently passed over. The paper's frame is cortical hierarchies plus thalamus; hippocampal anchor selection is not discussed, and so the symmetric-V_s-application claim of MECH-269b cannot be confirmed or denied from this evidence -- it is a measurement gap.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Hierarchical predictive processing requires a substrate that orchestrates and contextualises message passing across cortical areas. Kanai et al. argue this substrate is the pulvinar, which delivers second-order modulatory gain to cortical pyramidal cells reporting prediction error. The pulvinar's job is precision -- the salience or context ascribed to content -- and its anatomy supports a separation between driving (first-order, content-carrying) and modulatory (second-order, precision-carrying) extrinsic connections. Feature-ground segregation simulations demonstrate the computational consequences.
mapping.ree_translation: Kanai et al. supply the most direct biological precedent for the MECH-269b cortex-side gate. The pulvinar-cortex precision channel is the substrate-equivalent of MECH-269b's V_s applied to E1 / E2 streams. The two-stream architecture (driving content vs modulatory precision) maps directly onto MECH-269b's separation of stream content (the latent z) from stream gain (V_s). The proposal that precision is set externally to the receiving cortical area -- by a thalamic substrate -- mirrors REE's design where V_s is computed in a separate verisimilitude-tracking module and broadcast to consumers.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The granularity in Kanai et al. is per cortical region, not per content stream within a region. MECH-269b's claim is that precision can be set independently for each latent stream (z_world, z_self, etc.) which corresponds to different anatomical territories under REE's mapping but may include sub-regional resolution that the pulvinar literature does not address. Also, the paper does not engage with hippocampal anchor selection, so the symmetric-application claim remains untested in this paper specifically.
mapping.source_context: Major Phil Trans B review by Friston and collaborators, synthesising primate anatomy of pulvinar-cortex projections with the predictive-processing computational framework.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high (top-tier theoretical review, anatomically grounded). Mapping fidelity high because pulvinar-cortex precision gating is essentially the cortical specialisation of what MECH-269b proposes; transfer risk low because the mechanism is broadly cortical and applies across sensory and association areas.
